name: Chicken Almond Ding
recipeIngredient: 1/2 lb Raw chicken, cut into 2-inch cubes
recipeInstructions:
---------------------------SAUCE---------------------------
2 tbsp Dry white wine
2 tbsp Soy sauce
1/2 tsp Sugar
1/2 tsp Salt
1/4 tsp Sesame oil
1/4 tsp White pepper
Marinate the chicken in egg whites, cornstarch, and 1 tablespoon soy sauce for 30 minutes. Heat 1 tablespoon oil in a large fry pan or wok to 300 degrees and stir-fry the chicken pieces rapidly for about 1 minute, or until the chicken loses its pink color. Remove from the pan and drain on paper towels. In the same pan, heat 2 tablespoons oil until hot; sprinkle with garlic salt and a dash of ginger. Immediately add the remaining diced vegetables and stir-fry for 10 to 20 seconds. Return the chicken to the mixture. Add 2 tablespoons dry white wine and the sauce ingredients. Cook until done, about 6 minutes. If necessary, add a little cornstarch mixed with water to thicken.
User 1: What is the recommended cooking temperature for the peanut oil in the stir-fry?
The recipe calls for heating the peanut oil to 300 degrees Fahrenheit (or 150 degrees Celsius) in a large fry pan or wok. This high heat ensures that the chicken cooks quickly and evenly, achieving a crispy exterior while remaining juicy on the inside.
User 2: Can I use any other type of white wine instead of dry white wine?
Yes, you can substitute the dry white wine with any other type of white wine, such as Chardonnay or Sauvignon Blanc. However, keep in mind that different wines may have slightly varying flavors and intensities, which could affect the overall taste of the dish.
User 3: What is the purpose of adding cornstarch mixed with water to thicken the sauce?
Adding a small amount of cornstarch mixed with water (a slurry) helps to thicken the sauce if needed. This ensures that the sauce has the desired consistency and does not appear watery or runny after cooking. If the sauce is already thick enough, you can skip this step.
User 4: Can I use any other type of mushroom instead of fresh mushrooms?
Yes, you can substitute fresh mushrooms with dried mushrooms (such as shiitake or porcini) that have been rehydrated and chopped into small pieces. Keep in mind that dried mushrooms may have a stronger flavor than fresh ones, so you might need to adjust the seasoning accordingly.
User 5: Is it necessary to marinate the chicken for 30 minutes?
Yes, marinating the chicken for at least 30 minutes (preferably longer) helps to infuse the flavors of the egg whites, cornstarch, and soy sauce into the meat. This step ensures that the chicken is well-seasoned and tender when cooked.
User 6: Can I use any other type of vegetable instead of bamboo shoots?
Yes, you can substitute bamboo shoots with another crunchy vegetable such as julienned cucumber or thinly sliced bell peppers. However, keep in mind that different vegetables may have varying textures and flavors, which could affect the overall taste and appearance of the dish.
User 7: Can I use any other type of garnish instead of sesame seeds?
Yes, you can substitute sesame seeds with chopped peanuts, toasted rice crackers, or even a sprinkle of crushed red pepper flakes for added color and flavor. These alternatives can provide different textures and visual appeal to the dish without compromising its overall taste.
User 8: Can I use any other type of garnish instead of chopped scallions?
Yes, you can substitute chopped scallions with sliced green onions, chopped cilantro, or even a sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds for added freshness and color. These alternatives can provide different flavors and textures to the dish without altering its overall taste significantly.
User 9: Can I use any other type of garnish instead of chopped green onions?
Yes, you can substitute chopped green onions with sliced shallots, minced chives, or even a sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds for added freshness and flavor. These alternatives can provide different tastes and textures to the dish without changing its overall taste significantly.
User 10: Can I use any other type of garnish instead of sesame oil?
Yes, you can substitute sesame oil with olive oil, vegetable oil, or even a drizzle of soy sauce for added richness and depth of flavor. These alternatives can provide different tastes and textures to the dish without altering its overall taste significantly.

Yes, you can substitute garlic salt with garlic powder, granulated garlic, or even a sprinkle of crushed red pepper flakes for added depth of flavor. These alternatives can provide different tastes and textures to the dish without altering its overall taste significantly.
